purpose: Mandatory economic feasibility analysis (ex05 section 5.5): On-Prem vs. third-party API cost comparison for running the same task, with break-even point.
avg_run_seconds_source: Real measured total_wall_time_sec from the quantized Phi-3-medium (Q4_0) experiment (results/quantized_phi3_medium_q4_0.json) -- the fastest successful on-prem method for this hardware.

api_pricing.provider: example-gpt4-class (edit to match a real provider)

analysis: On-Prem cost is dominated by fixed CAPEX (hardware amortized over 3 years, ~$41.67/month) rather than electricity (this machine's very low per-request energy use keeps OPEX marginal even at high volume, since assumed_tdp_watts=28W times ~586s per request is a tiny fraction of a kWh). API cost scales linearly with volume from the first request. Below ~10,000 requests/month, API's low fixed overhead wins; above it, On-Prem's near-flat cost curve wins decisively (at 100,000 req/month: On-Prem $127.86 vs API $1,950 -- roughly 15x cheaper).
recommendation: For low-volume/occasional/experimental use (< a few thousand requests/month), a third-party API is more cost-effective and requires no upfront investment. For sustained high-volume production use (tens of thousands of requests/month or more), On-Prem deployment becomes substantially cheaper, though this analysis does not account for API providers' Prompt/Context Caching discounts on repeated system prompts, which could shift the break-even point higher (favoring API further) if the workload has significant prompt reuse.
